topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The heavy oak door clicked shut with a finality that scraped against my nerves. I stood in the echoing silence of the office, the scent of aged paper and expensive leather suddenly suffocating. "Gone?" I managed, the word sounding brittle and foreign in the quiet space. I turned back to the empty desk, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. Thirty years. Thirty years of late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee and shared ambition, and this was the curtain call? I paced the length of the room, my footsteps sharp on the polished floor, a low, frustrated rumble building in my chest. He hadn't even bothered to write a note. Not a single courtesy. It was an insult, a complete dismissal of everything we had built. I slammed my palm flat onto the mahogany surface, the sound sharp enough to startle the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light.

[story 2]

I stared at the framed photograph on his desk—a slightly faded picture of us from a conference years ago—and felt a strange, hot pressure behind my eyes. Retirement. That was the official word. But the way he’d simply vanished, leaving behind this vacuum of silence, felt like a deliberate slight. I picked up the thick file he’d left open, my fingers trembling slightly as I ran them over the embossed title. "Excuse me?" I called out, my voice pitched higher than I intended, laced with an edge that hadn't been there moments before. When no response came, I let the file drop onto the desk with a decisive thud. I crossed my arms tightly across my chest, the muscles in my shoulders bunching up. This couldn't be right. After all the sacrifices, the late nights spent honing my craft under his tutelage, he just… evaporated.

[story 3]

The air in the study felt thick, stagnant, refusing to move. I stood by the window, watching the city rush by, feeling a sudden, sharp clenching in my stomach. He was gone. Just… gone. No farewell, no final word, just an empty chair where his imposing presence used to sit. I turned slowly, my gaze sweeping over the room as if searching for some hidden explanation, some justification for this abrupt severance. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. "What kind of professional courtesy is this?" I muttered to the empty room, the tone clipped and utterly devoid of warmth. It felt like a betrayal, a careless erasure of a significant chapter, and the sheer lack of acknowledgement made my throat feel tight with unshed frustration.
